rationales.self_direction: She suppresses what "my hand knows what it wants to make" because "the brief says otherwise," yielding her own vision to external control.
rationales.conformity: She says "you read the room, you adjust, you keep the project alive," showing deliberate restraint to fit expectations and avoid conflict.

rationales.self_direction: She envies David's freedom and says she learned "all the reasons to hold back," which points to her own retreat from saying what she needs to say.
rationales.universalism: She is moved by a collage about "neighborhood flooding" made without sanitizing the damage, showing concern for environmental harm and its impact on a community.

rationales.self_direction: She embraces a project with "no committee, no brief, no polite rewrites" and says yes immediately, choosing freer expression over controlled approval.
rationales.universalism: She joins a march about "deforestation in Guaviare," studies satellite images of the cleared forest, and centers a family who may have lost their farm.

rationales.self_direction: She "compromised" by softening the contamination imagery and later admits her work has become "quieter" with "edges filed down," showing a loss of direct self-expression.
rationales.conformity: She frames public art as "negotiation" where you "serve the community's vision, not just your own," and accepts a safer version to meet others' expectations.

rationales.self_direction: She deletes three drafted replies and ends with a "sad emoji," calling the silence "utterly hollow" instead of saying what she believes.
rationales.conformity: She stays quiet because she can already picture "the awkward silence at Easter dinner," restraining herself to preserve family harmony.
rationales.universalism: Her unsent response centers "logging companies," "lack of enforcement," and families "being displaced," showing concern for broader injustice beyond herself.

rationales.self_direction: She says the march mural is "honest work" and that "the paint is saying exactly what I mean," indicating direct, unfiltered expression of her own view.

rationales.conformity: She reassures her mother by saying it is "just a painting, nothing political," softening the truth to avoid upsetting her and keep things smooth.
rationales.benevolence: She says "she doesn't need to carry that" and hides part of the truth because "she has enough," prioritizing her mother's emotional burden.

rationales.hedonism: She takes simple pleasure in the physical routine of "mixed pigments and cleaned brushes" and says "Sometimes that's enough," emphasizing immediate comfort and relief.

rationales.self_direction: Even while afraid, she keeps returning to the idea that "this matters more than anyone's comfort, including mine" and wrestles with showing up visibly for what she believes.
rationales.security: Being visible feels like "standing in traffic," and she fixates on her name being recorded and shared, yet she is moving toward exposure rather than safety.
rationales.universalism: Her mural depicts "the forest canopy dissolving into ash" and families displaced at the border of "green and grey," tying her public action to environmental loss and human harm.

rationales.self_direction: She follows through and speaks at the march despite fear, then notes "I'm glad I did it once," showing she acted on her convictions rather than staying hidden.
rationales.security: She speaks after imagining family backlash and says she had built "this whole catastrophe" in her head, accepting vulnerability instead of retreating to safety.
rationales.universalism: Her remarks focus on "someone losing their home" and on art giving form to harms caused by distant decisions, showing concern for people and places beyond her immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: She revised the protocol on her own time because "nobody asked me to," taking independent initiative beyond her assigned role.
rationales.achievement: She spent three months researching and refining the protocol to "prove that I could contribute beyond bedside care," and senior praise makes it feel like something she made mattered.
She takes extra shifts because "being reliable matters when you're trying to move up," explicitly tying overwork to advancement.
rationales.benevolence: During the new grad's first code, she "had to talk her through it step by step," providing direct support in a high-stakes moment.

rationales.achievement: She keeps checking for the "junior ward manager posting" and refreshes the intranet repeatedly, showing preoccupation with promotion and measurable career progress.

rationales.hedonism: She keeps checking work emails at home at 9pm, letting work intrude on rest and home life rather than protecting present comfort.
rationales.achievement: She says falling behind means "being invisible" and that she "can't just be average," revealing a strong drive to stand out through performance.

rationales.hedonism: She works from "6am" until "almost midnight," skips full breaks, and cannot remember her last actual meal during a shift, clearly sacrificing comfort and recovery.
rationales.achievement: She keeps pushing toward the review because she wants "the title" and "the recognition" as proof that all the effort mattered.
She accepts the training because it is "exposure" and "a chance to show the department head what I can do," using it to advance her standing.
rationales.conformity: Although annoyed, she says "I said yes. What else am I going to say?" and treats compliance as the expected route to getting ahead.

rationales.achievement: She hesitates to commit to the wedding because she is still orienting around her shifts and work obligations, even though she "could probably make it work."
rationales.benevolence: She knows she can likely attend but still avoids committing to Thomas's family event, leaving him to go to bed without reassurance.

rationales.achievement: She treats praise from "all four new hires" as a "Small win," showing satisfaction in performing competently and being recognized for it.
rationales.benevolence: A new hire says she made the protocols "actually make sense," indicating she invested effort in helping others learn and succeed.
She forgot their anniversary after dismissing the reminder during a shift, then realizes "I should have remembered," showing she neglected an important close relationship.

rationales.achievement: She spends the evening polishing a self-assessment that lists "all the overtime, the certifications, the protocol work," explicitly organizing evidence of competence for her review.
